evidence_quotes.quote: video games being "killed" or made completely unplayable after their online servers are shut down or support ends.
The gaming industry is pushing more and more for consumers to only have "licences" for their purchased games even when it's a physical copy of the game.

paragraphs.text: What needs to be addressed and changed is the practice of video games being "killed" or made completely unplayable after their online servers are shut down or support ends. When I buy a video game, whether it's a physical copy or a digital download, I see it as owning a product. It's something I've spent my hard-earned money on, and I expect to be able to play it. But increasingly, what happens is that game publishers decide to turn off the servers for older games, and suddenly, the game I bought becomes a useless piece of data or plastic. It's like buying a book, and then a few years later, the publisher comes to your house and burns it because they no longer want to support it, or they want you to read one of their new books instead. This isn't fair. It feels like my ownership is being revoked, and the money I spent is simply wasted. It's a huge gap in consumer protection in the digital space. We're told we "own" these games, but if they can be taken away from us at any moment, do we really? The gaming industry is pushing more and more for consumers to only have "licences" for their purchased games even when it's a physical copy of the game. I strongly urge the Commission to include clear rules in the Digital Fairness Act that prevent this practice from happening any longer. Consumers should have the right to keep playing the games they've purchased, even if the original company decides to stop supporting the online features. This could mean requiring developers to provide an offline mode, or perhaps releasing tools that allow communities to keep games alive on their own servers. There must be a way to ensure that our digital purchases have lasting value. I believe addressing this "killing of games" is absolutely crucial for truly protecting consumers in our digital future. Some gamers myself included are already going back to retro gaming consoles and retro games because they can truly own those and play them whenever they want without the need for an online connection, and their gaming console can't be blocked and rendered useless (unlike, for example, a Nintendo Switch 2) which is in itself another problem and practice that should be stopped immediately.
